title: Nimrod: The Mighty Hunter Who Founded Ethiopia
subtitle: 3776 BC — The Journey from Babel to the Horn of Africa

sections.title: The Tribe of Ori or Aram
sections.body:
The 1922 Ethiopian Regnal List places Nimrod as the twelfth sovereign of the 'Tribe of Ori or Aram' — the first post-diluvian dynasty to rule over Ethiopia. This dynasty, consisting of 21 sovereigns, reigned from 4470 to 3244 BC, spanning over a millennium. Ori or Aram himself reigned for 60 years, followed by a succession of rulers whose names echo through Ethiopian tradition: Gariak I, Gannkam, Queen Borsa, Gariak II, Djan I, Djan II, Senefrou, Zeenabzamin, Sahlan, and Elaryan.

Nimrod's reign is recorded as lasting 60 years, beginning in the year 3776 BC — a date that places him firmly within the ancient Near Eastern world. This detailed chronology demonstrates the sophistication of Ethiopian historical traditions, which sought to create a continuous narrative connecting the nation's origins to the broader biblical history.
Cush begat Nimrod: he began to be a mighty one in the earth. He was a mighty hunter before the Lord.

sections.title: Nimrod's Divine Mission
sections.body:
Ethiopian tradition presents Nimrod's migration to the Horn of Africa not as conquest but as a divinely ordained quest. According to the narrative preserved in Ethiopian chronicles, Nimrod — having established his kingdom in Mesopotamia and built the city of Babel — prayed to God, asking for his own land. In response, God commanded him: 'Go and search for it — go get the land of Adam.'

This divine instruction set Nimrod on an epic journey. Accompanied by 47 vassal kings, he first traveled to Gaza on the Mediterranean coast before turning southward, crossing vast deserts and mountain ranges until he reached the highlands of northern Ethiopia. This was no ordinary migration — it was a sacred mission to find and claim a promised land.

sections.title: Settlement at Axum, Waldeba, and Gihon
sections.body:
Upon reaching Ethiopia, Nimrod established a series of settlements that would become the spiritual and political heartland of Ethiopian civilization. His first stop was at Axum — the site that would later become the capital of one of the ancient world's greatest empires. Ethiopian tradition holds that Nimrod recognized the strategic and spiritual significance of this location immediately.

From Axum, Nimrod moved to Waldeba, a region in present-day northwestern Ethiopia, expanding his domain into the highlands. However, his final and most significant settlement was at Gihon — identified in the biblical Book of Genesis as one of the four rivers flowing from Eden, and equated in Ethiopian tradition with the Abay River (the Blue Nile) at its source at Gish Abay.

The choice of Gihon is deeply symbolic. By settling at this sacred river, Nimrod connected his new kingdom directly to the Garden of Eden itself, reinforcing the Ethiopian belief that their land is one of divine blessing and primordial sanctity. Ethiopian tradition holds that Nimrod spent the rest of his life at Gihon, and some believe his grave remains at Axum to this day.

sections.title: Gera: The Wise Son of Nimrod
sections.body:
Nimrod's son Gera (also spelled Gira) inherited both his father's kingdom and his wisdom. Ethiopian chronicles describe Gera as a powerful and learned ruler who possessed the secrets of the prophet Enoch and commanded vast territories extending from Ethiopia to the Middle East, including Gaza. Gera is credited with remarkable architectural achievements — the construction of pyramids, obelisks, and palaces that rivaled the great civilizations of Egypt and Nubia.

Legend holds that Gera named the city of Jerusalem after his wife, Salem — a claim that, while historically unverifiable, creates a direct link between the Ethiopian royal line and the Holy City. This connection would later be reinforced through the Solomonic dynasty's claim of descent from King Solomon and the Queen of Sheba.

In his old age, Gera divided his vast empire among his three sons: Ethiel, Hamun, and Flestha. He bestowed upon them the symbols of their authority — including the 'great Stave of the Ankh' — and appointed them as both kings and priests over different parts of his domain. This story serves to explain the origins of the various kingdoms and cultures of the region, from Ethiopia to Sudan and Egypt.

sections.title: Ethiel and the Naming of Ethiopia
sections.body:
Among Gera's three sons, Ethiel received the most prominent inheritance: the Ethiopian highlands themselves. Ethiopian tradition holds that the very name of the country derives from a combination of 'Ethiel' and 'Yop' — the ancient name for the region. While modern linguistics does not support this etymology, the narrative is deeply embedded in Ethiopian national identity, suggesting that the country's very name is rooted in the legacy of this legendary king.

Ethiel received the 'great Stave of the Ankh' as the symbol of supreme authority — the 'mark of the King of Kings' that signified his sovereignty over all his brothers. The Ankh, the ancient Egyptian symbol of life, is said to have originated with Ethiel's dynasty and spread from Ethiopia to Egypt and Sudan through his brothers Hamun and Flestha, who ruled those lands with lesser authority.

This traditional account reverses the conventional direction of cultural influence, positioning Ethiopia as the source of civilization rather than its recipient — a powerful expression of Ethiopian cultural pride that has resonated through centuries of national self-understanding.
From Ethiel and Yop came the name Ethiopia — a name that carries the weight of ancient kings and divine favor.
